coredata.dc:title: Tunable fluorescent biomass-derived carbon dots for efficient antibacterial action and bioimaging
coredata.prism:publicationName: Colloids and Surfaces A: Physicochemical and Engineering Aspects
coredata.dc:creator: Hua, Jianhao
Hua, Peng
Qin, Kunhao
coredata.dc:description: Carbon dots (CDs)-based antibacterial biomaterials offer great potential in addressing the severe threat of antibiotic-resistant bacteria to public health. Positively charged CDs usually adhere on bacterial membranes via electrostatic interactions, however, they may cause hemolysis and cytotoxicity, and dissatisfactory fluorescence emission restricts their applications in bioimaging. Herein, we developed a negatively charged tunable multicolor nitrogen and phosphorus co-doped CDs (N, P-CDs) from mango peel, a renewable food waste biomass. The N, P-CDs exhibit good biocompatibility and photostability, thus could be adopted to multicolor cell labeling in vitro. More importantly, the negatively charged N, P-CDs possess intrinsic antibacterial activity against E. coli and S. aureus, and antimicrobial mechanisms were investigated by real-time quantitative PCR (RT-qPCR). We found the different mechanisms of N, P-CDs against E. coli and S. aureus, the antibacterial mechanism of N, P-CDs against E. coli mainly involve inhibition of binary fission proliferation and transcription of genetic information, interference with carbon metabolism and sugar conversion, thus inducing growth arrest and cell death. For S. aureus, N, P-CDs could serve as quorum sensing (QS) inhibitor to disrupt QS system, inhibit biofilm formation, DNA replication, cell division, and signal transmission. Our study provides important insights into antibacterial mechanism of CDs at the molecular level, which may facilitate the further exploration and application of natural resources-based antibacterial biomaterials.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Biomass
Carbon dots
Multicolor bioimaging
Bacterial inhibition
Antibacterial mechanisms

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fabrication and chemical structures of N, P-CDs
Proposed inhibition mechanism of N, P-CDs

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Kunming University of Science and Technology

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by grants from the Analysis and Testing Foundation of Kunming University of Science and Technology (No. 2017M20162118079 ), and the China Postdoctoral Science Foundation (No. 2018M643535 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM images of (a) N-CDs, (b) N, P-CDs; HR-TEM images of (c) N-CDs, (d) N, P-CDs. (e) Raman spectra of the both CDs.
High-resolution XPS (a) N1s and (b) P2p spectra of N, P-CDs. (c) ζ potentials of N-CDs and N, P-CDs.

Optical properties of N, P-CDs: (a) FL excitation and emission spectra of N, P-CDs, (b) FL emission spectra of N, P-CDs under different excitation wavelengths, (c) FL decay plot of N, P-CDs, (d) FLQY of N-CDs and N, P-CDs, (e) UV–vis absorption spectra of N-CDs and N, P-CDs, insets: the photographs of N-CDs and N, P-CDs solution under room-light (left) and UV light of 365 nm (right), (f) the schematic of π-π * and n-π * transition in N, P-CDs.
CLSM fluorescence images of HeLa cells incubated with different concentrations of N, P-CDs (20, 40, 80 and 120 μg mL−1), the scale bar is 20 µm.

In vitro antibacterial performance of N, P-CDs: growth curves of (a) E. coli and (b) S. aureus treated with different concentrations of N, P-CDs (0, 5, 30, and 60 μg mL−1), (c) photographs of bacterial colonies formed by E. coli and S. aureus treated with different concentrations of N, P-CDs, (d) SEM images of E. coli and S. aureus after treatments with or without N, P-CDs, the scale bar is 1 µm.
CLSM fluorescence images of (a) E. coli and (b) S. aureus incubated with N, P-CDs for 30 min and 60 min, respectively. The scale bar is 10 µm.

Antibacterial mechanisms of N, P-CDs. The expression level of related genes of (a) E. coli and (b) S. aureus after treatments with or without N, P-CDs. Biofilm inhibition of N, P-CDs: (c) images of biofilm biomass stained by crystal violet upon different treatments, (d) quantitative analysis of the biofilm biomass by UV absorption at 570 nm, (e) quantitative analysis of the leakage of intracellular DNA upon different treatments. (n.s., p > 0.05; *** p < 0.001).
Schematic diagram of the preparation process of N, P-CDs.
Graphical description of different antibacterial mechanisms of N, P-CDs against E. coli (G-) and S. aureus (G+).

head.ce:title: Tunable fluorescent biomass-derived carbon dots for efficient antibacterial action and bioimaging

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry, State Key Laboratory of Elemento-Organic Chemistry, Key Laboratory of Advanced Energy Materials Chemistry (Ministry of Education), Nankai University, Tianjin 300071, China

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Faculty of Life Science and Technology, Kunming University of Science and Technology, Kunming, Yunnan Province 650500, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Faculty of Life Science and Technology, Kunming University of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Faculty of Life Science and Technology, Kunming University of Science and Technology, Kunming, Yunnan Province 650500, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Third People's Hospital of Yunnan Province, Kunming, Yunnan Province 650011, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Third People's Hospital of Yunnan Province

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Third People's Hospital of Yunnan Province, Kunming, Yunnan Province, 650011, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon dots (CDs)-based antibacterial biomaterials offer great potential in addressing the severe threat of antibiotic-resistant bacteria to public health. Positively charged CDs usually adhere on bacterial membranes via electrostatic interactions, however, they may cause hemolysis and cytotoxicity, and dissatisfactory fluorescence emission restricts their applications in bioimaging. Herein, we developed a negatively charged tunable multicolor nitrogen and phosphorus co-doped CDs (N, P-CDs) from mango peel, a renewable food waste biomass. The N, P-CDs exhibit good biocompatibility and photostability, thus could be adopted to multicolor cell labeling in vitro. More importantly, the negatively charged N, P-CDs possess intrinsic antibacterial activity against E. coli and S. aureus, and antimicrobial mechanisms were investigated by real-time quantitative PCR (RT-qPCR). We found the different mechanisms of N, P-CDs against E. coli and S. aureus, the antibacterial mechanism of N, P-CDs against E. coli mainly involve inhibition of binary fission proliferation and transcription of genetic information, interference with carbon metabolism and sugar conversion, thus inducing growth arrest and cell death. For S. aureus, N, P-CDs could serve as quorum sensing (QS) inhibitor to disrupt QS system, inhibit biofilm formation, DNA replication, cell division, and signal transmission. Our study provides important insights into antibacterial mechanism of CDs at the molecular level, which may facilitate the further exploration and application of natural resources-based antibacterial biomaterials.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Bacterial infection-related diseases have become a serious threat to human health and global economics. Currently, antibiotics are the main therapeutic strategy for fighting against bacterial infections [1,2]. Unfortunately, long-term overuse of traditional antibiotics have led to the development of antibiotic tolerance and resistance, antibiotic-resistant bacteria are causing a global health crisis [3]. In response, alternative antibacterial agents and technologies have been actively studied. The antibacterial modalities of emerging nanomaterials are novel for pathogenic bacteria [4], thus, various nanomaterials have been explored as available alternatives to combat antibiotic-resistant infections [5–7]. Among them, carbon-based nanomaterials, including 2D materials [8], 1D nanotubes [9], and 0D carbon dots (CDs) [10] have been extensively explored in recent years due to their relatively higher biosafety and unique physicochemical characteristics. Comparing reported carbon nanomaterials, 0D CDs have attracted wider attention due to its broad-spectrum antimicrobial activity, higher efficiency, and facile delivery [10,11]. As is well known, the bacterial surface of both Gram-negative (G-) and Gram-positive (G+) bacteria is negatively charged [12]. Thus, positively charged CDs have been extensively explored to adhere on targeted bacteria via electrostatic interactions [11]. However, they can cause undesired hemolysis and cytotoxicity since most biological membranes are mainly composed of negatively charged phospholipid bilayers [13]. On the other hand, the antibacterial mechanism of CDs mainly focuses on the generation of reactive oxygen species (ROS), photoactivated activity, and disruption of cell membrane [10,14,15]. Unfortunately, these mechanisms cannot further elaborate the signaling pathway of antibacterial CDs against bacteria at molecular level, and there is still a lack of in-depth knowledge of the biological actions between CDs and bacteria. Therefore, it is imperative and meaningful to develop negatively charged CDs with good biocompatibility and efficient antibacterial activity, as well clarify the molecular mechanism of CDs against bacteria.

In addition to the field of antibacterial applications, CDs also exhibit great potential in other biomedical application. However, most reported CDs show blue or green fluorescence (FL) emission, which significantly restricts their applications in bioimaging and biosensing duo to the high interference from tissue auto-fluorescence [16–18]. Therefore, there is urgent desire to attain long-wavelength, multicolor, and tunable FL CDs for further applications. To this end, many strategies have been adopted to design and fabricate multicolor CDs with organic molecules as precursors, such as doping heteroatoms, controlling particle size, engineering surface state [19–21]. It has been proven that carbon precursors play an important role in regulating optical properties of CDs, besides organic molecules, syntheses to obtain multicolor CDs from biomass is equally attractive [22,23]. In this regard, biomass contain abundant carbohydrate, thus can be selected as a green and renewable natural carbon resources for the preparation of CDs [19,24]. Moreover, waste biomass is considered the most environmentally friendly and low-cost carbon precursors, and there is still enormous space to explore the preparation of biomass-derived CDs [25,26]. Therefore, developing a facile strategy to prepare biomass-derived multicolor CDs and explore the multifaceted applications in biological systems are exceedingly attractive.

Motivated by the above issues, we synthesized a nitrogen and phosphorus co-doped CDs (N, P-CDs) by simple microwave-assisted method with mango peel as precursor. As a waste biomass, mango peel is produced from daily life and has been classified as a widespread wet waste, and the green synthesis method is eco-friendly and low-cost. The obtained N, P-CDs exhibit excellent optical properties, including tunable multicolor FL emission and good photostability, thus could be adopted to multicolor cell imaging. More importantly, the negatively charged N, P-CDs possess intrinsic antibacterial activity against E. coli (G-) and S. aureus (G+), the effects of N, P-CDs on the related genes of both E. coli and S. aureus were investigated by real-time quantitative PCR (RT-qPCR). We found that N, P-CDs against E. coli and S. aureus had different antibacterial mechanisms at the molecular level, which is conducive to the further design and application of carbon-based antibacterial agents.

The mango peel as precursor was applied to synthesize CDs via a simple one-step microwave method here. As illustrated in Scheme 1, mango peel was separated from fresh mangoes, washed with deionized water, followed by drying naturally under room temperature, and ground into powder. Then, 0.5 g powder was added into the 20 mL phosphoric acid solution, the mixture was ultrasonicated for 10 min after it was stirred evenly. Subsequently, the solution was put into a domestic microwave oven for continuous heating and then allowed to cool to room temperature. Obtained mixture was first filtered to remove solid residues, the filtrate was then neutralized with NaOH (0.1 M). The resultant dispersion was filtered again through a 220 nm microporous membrane, and then centrifuged at 11000 rpm for 15 min. Finally, the obtained brown CDs solution was stored at 4 ℃ or freeze-dried to solid powder for further characterization.

For the growth-inhibition experiments, G- E. coli (BW 25113) and G+ S. aureus (ATCC 25923) were used in this work. The single colonies of E. coli and S. aureus were obtained via streak plate method. The single colonies were grown in liquid LB medium overnight (12 h) in a 37 °C incubator shaker (150 rpm). In order to obtained the growth curves of both strains, overnight cultures were inoculated into the fresh LB medium, and N, P-CDs with different concentrations (5 μg mL−1, 30 μg mL−1, 60 μg mL−1) were added. Control groups were prepared by adding the equivalent volume of sterile water. The turbidity of bacteria suspension (absorbance at 600 nm, OD600) was measured at different time points. In addition, the treated suspension that cultured for 6 h were diluted 1000 times before plate coating, all solid agar plates were incubated at 37 °C for 12 h, and the colony count was observed. To explore the effect of N, P-CDs on the bacterial morphology, both strains treated by N, P-CDs for 6 h and control groups were examined using scanning electron microscopy (SEM).

The RT-qPCR assay was applied to study the relative expression levels of E. coli genes (waaC, ftsA, ftsZ, rpoA, rpoB, csrA, ptsH, ptsI, holA, dnaE and waaA) and S. aureus genes (icaA, agrA, ftsA, polA, dnaE, rpoB and rpoA). Here, RT-qPCR was performed in a LineGene 9600 Plus real-time PCR detection system. The gene-specific primer sequences used in this study are listed in Table S1 and Table S2. Quantitative PCR mixtures were set up in 12 μL containing 0.1 μM primers and SYBR green qPCR mix with the following program: 5 min at 95 °C for denaturation, followed by 40 cycles of 20 s at 95 °C, 20 s at 55 °C, and 20 s at 72 °C. Melting curve analysis was performed from 60 °C to 95 °C with an increment of 1 °C for 20 s. The comparative threshold cycle (Ct) method was used for relative quantification of RNA. The expression levels of waaA and rpoA as the endogenous control for E. coli and S. aureus, respectively.

The bacteria suspension of E. coli and S. aureus (2 mL, OD600 = 0.6) were centrifuged at 10000 g for 5 min, the remaining bacteria precipitates were washed thrice with PBS buffer and resuspended in 1 mL PBS solution. Then N, P-CDs was added to the bacteria suspension with a final concentration of 30 μg mL−1 and incubated at 37 °C. After incubation with a predetermined time, the stained bacteria were washed with PBS buffer and imaged by confocal laser scanning microscopy (CLSM). As a reference, G+ Bacillus cereus (B. cereus) was also stained and imaged with the same process.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication and chemical structures of N, P-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The N-CDs and N, P-CDs were synthesized through a bottom-up route based on a facile microwave-assisted method (Scheme 1). Compared with common hydrothermal methods, our method greatly shortens reaction time and reduces energy consumption. Meanwhile, as a low-cost and sustainable raw material, mango peel is a promising precursor for preparing N-CDs through self-doping during CDs formation. Furthermore, it has been proved that co-doping multiplex heteroatoms in CDs is an effective functionalization strategy due to the synergistic effect between the doped heteroatoms [27]. Considering that N and P are in close proximity to C in periodic table, N and P co-doping can alter the surface characteristics and inherent electronic properties of CDs [28,29]. Inspired by this, phosphoric acid was used as a doping agent and reaction solvent for the preparation of N, P-CDs in our work. Basically, the FL quantum yield (FLQY) of N, P-CDs was affected by microwave radiation time and concentration of H3PO4. In order to obtain acceptable FLQY, we optimized the above experimental conditions: the reaction time of 10 min (Fig. S1a) and 10 % H3PO4 solution (Fig. S1b) were chosen as the optimized condition. Therefore, it can be considered that maintaining the doping balance between N and P is essential for a high FLQY.

The morphology and structure of the synthesized CDs were characterized via transmission electron microscopy (TEM). As shown in Fig. 1a, N-CDs are monodispersed spherical dots with average diameter of 5.68 ± 0.52 nm (Fig. S2a). Interestingly, the N, P-CDs remained well dispersed (Fig. 1b) but the average diameter decreased to 4.16 ± 0.58 nm (Fig. S2b) after P doping. Meanwhile, the HR-TEM images (Fig. 1c-d) exhibit the high crystallinity of both CDs, and distinct lattice fringes with lattice spacing of 0.2 nm (N-CDs) and 0.23 nm (N, P-CDs), corresponding to the (100) in-plane lattice of graphitic (sp 2) carbon [30]. It could be inferred that graphitization process occurred during the formation of both CDs. In addition, Raman spectra was applied to further investigate the graphitization degree of both CDs. Two typical Raman peaks (Fig. 1e) were exhibited at 1360.5 cm−1 and 1565.6 cm−1, corresponding to D band (disorder in sp 3 carbon) and G band (graphitic domains in sp 2 carbon), respectively [31]. The ratio of I
D
/I
G is 0.95, and 0.84 for N-CDs and N, P-CDs, respectively. The decrease ratio values imply the increased graphite content from N-CDs to N, P-CDs, suggesting the graphitization of N, P-CDs was enhanced after P doping [18]. Accordingly, It could be concluded that P-dopant does not destroy the formation of carbon nuclei, but inhibits the growth of small dots into larger carbon particles [32]. Based on the above characterization results, we then investigated the structure of N, P-CDs through X-ray diffraction (XRD). A distinct diffraction peak centered at around 21.2° (Fig. S3) confirms the characteristic (002) lattice spacing of graphite structure of N, P-CDs and high crystallinity [33], which is consistent with the TEM results.

We further investigated the surface composition of N, P-CDs by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) characterization. The N, P-CDs mainly contain C, N, O, and P elements, as seen from the full XPS spectrum (Fig. S4). The C1s spectrum (Fig. S5a) can be deconvoluted into four kinds of C species, corresponding to sp 2 C (CC) at 283.2 eV, sp 3 C (C-C and C-P) at 284.0 eV, nitrous C (C-O and C-N) at 284.8 eV, and carbonyl or carboxyl C (-CO) at 287.3 eV, respectively. Here, the bonds between C and the heteroatoms reflected the doping of N and P atoms. The O1s spectrum (Fig. S5b) was deconvoluted into three peaks: the C-O/P-O (530.1 eV), CO/P = O (531.4 eV), and C-OH/P-OH (532.9 eV). For the doped N and P components, N1s spectrum ( Fig. 2a) can be converted into C-N-C (396.9 eV), N-(C)3 (398.0 eV), and N-H (399.5 eV), respectively. The P2p spectrum (Fig. 2b) reveals the presence of P-O (132.5 eV) and P-C (133.9 eV). The above results confirm N and P were incorporated into the sp 2-carbon domains, and the surface was functionalized by heteroatom- and oxygen-containing groups [34,35]. The ζ potential values of both CDs in water (Fig. 2c) were measured to be − 9.9, and − 17.8 mV, respectively. As expected, the negative potential indicates the presence of negatively charged functional groups on the surface of CDs, and more negative potential of N, P-CDs is attributed to P doping [36].

Based on the above structure characterization, we speculate that N, P-CDs might exhibit distinctive optical properties differed from conventional blue fluorescent CDs. Therefore, further investigation was conducted on its optical properties. As shown in Fig. 3a, the FL spectra of aqueous N, P-CDs show a maximum emission peak at 499 nm under optimal excitation wavelength (434 nm). Strikingly, the N, P-CDs exhibits nearly continuous excitation-dependent FL behavior. The FL of N, P-CDs can be excited as altering the excitation wavelengths from 330 to 470 nm, and the emission peaks cover most of the visible region (Fig. 3b). We can observe apparent blue to red fluorescence within a wide range of excitation wavelengths, the tunable multicolor emission feature is more advantageous than most CDs reported in previous studies emit blue or green light. Additionally, the FL spectra show broad and contain multiple emission under short excitation wavelength, it indicates that generated emissions may further (partially) act as excitation source and then inducing multiple emissions [37]. The difference in bandwidth of emission spectra could be attributed to the complicated surface states and band gaps of N, P-CDs [38–40].

Generally, the luminescence and FLQY of CDs are related to electronic transition process of surface states [41]. As shown in Fig. 3c, the time-resolved FL decay curve can be fitted by a double-exponential function, with a 3.31 ns lifetime. The FL decay curve includes a fast component (τ
1 =2.01 ns) owing to radiative recombination of the intrinsic states, and slower component (τ
2 =5.49 ns) due to surface groups. The FLQY were then measured and found to increase from 9.7% of N-CDs to 14.3 % of N, P-CDs (Fig. 3d). These results also indicate that the N, P-CDs possesses diverse fluorophores or energy levels, resulting in multiple decay channels [42]. Subsequently, UV–vis absorption spectra of both CDs were investigated. As shown in Fig. 3e, the N, P-CDs solution is light brown under the room-light and exhibits bright yellowish-green fluorescence under a 365 nm UV light, with an evident red shift compared to the blue fluorescence of N-CDs, suggesting that the P doping play a key role in producing long-wavelength emissions. The UV–vis absorption spectrum of N, P-CDs demonstrates the presence of multiple electronic absorption transitions (depicted in Fig. 3f). Interestingly, the N, P-CDs exhibits an intense absorption band at 206 nm, with an obvious blue shift compared to the 226 nm of N-CDs, assigned to the π-π * transition of aromatic sp 2 domains (CC). Another absorption band at 283 nm can be ascribed to n-π * transition of CN/CO bonds. Besides, the observed stronger absorption bands in ultraviolet (UV) region for N, P-CDs could be attributed to the increased graphite content, sp 2 domain and the formation of more conjugated structures due to the N and P co-doping [43–45]. On the basis of the above results, the N, P-CDs exhibits tunable multicolor emissions, which is beneficial for efficient multicolor bioimaging under different excitation channels.

Encouraged by the excellent optical properties mentioned above, we adopted N, P-CDs as a bio-label, and explored the feasibility for cellular imaging. First, the in vitro cytotoxicity was preliminarily evaluated by MTT assay with HeLa cells, HT-29 cells and U-2 OS cells. Over 90 % cell viabilities (Fig. S6) was observed for cells incubated with N, P-CDs even at high concentration (up to 200 μg mL−1), confirming low cytotoxicity and good in vitro biocompatibility of N, P-CDs. Additionally, we recorded the fluorescence imaging of cells incubated with N, P-CDs (80 μg mL−1) (Fig. S7), the three batches exhibited intense blue, green, and red fluorescence under different excitation channels, respectively. The images verify the good photostability and cell imaging capability of N, P-CDs. Subsequently, HeLa cells was used as a model to further investigate the multicolor labeling ability of N, P-CDs. As shown in Fig. 4, N, P-CDs penetrated all of the incubated HeLa cells, regardless of the concentration of N, P-CDs, and the FL signal locate nearly at the whole cellular regions, suggesting the N, P-CDs not only can penetrate cell membranes, but even enter into cell nuclei. The intracellular fluorescence intensity gradually increased with the concentration of N, P-CDs from 20 to 120 μg mL−1, indicating the excellent stability of N, P-CDs during cell internalization. The images of HeLa cells and FL signal under different excitation channels are also well merged with each other, thus indicating that the prepared N, P-CDs can be used as an effective multicolor fluorescent label in bioimaging.

It is evident that CDs and CDs-based composites exhibit potential bactericidal action.[10,11] Moreover, natural products extracted from fruit byproducts exhibit high antibacterial activity [46]. To explore the potential of the mango peel-derived N, P-CDs beyond multicolor bioimaging, we evaluated the antimicrobial activity of negatively charged N, P-CDs against two representative bacterial strains, i.e. E. coli and S. aureus.

To this end, the growth inhibition effect of N, P-CDs against both bacterial strains was preliminarily tested by bacterial growth curves. The bacterial growth was monitored over time after incubation with different concentrations of N, P-CDs, and the recorded OD600 values at various time points reflect the breeding processes of the bacteria. As shown in Fig. 5a, the growth inhibition ability against E. coli enhanced with increasing N, P-CDs concentrations. N, P-CDs started to slightly inhibit E. coli growth from 5 µg mL−1 after 4 h of incubation, and exhibited complete inhibition at 60 µg mL−1. Similar results were found in the S. aureus (Fig. 5b), the OD600 values of S. aureus suspension dramatically decreased when N, P-CDs (60 µg mL−1) was introduced, validating the excellent inhibition capability of N, P-CDs against both bacterial strains. The antibacterial activity of N, P-CDs was further confirmed using the plate counting method. As shown in Fig. 5c, compared with the control groups, the viability of both bacterial strains significantly reduced after incubation with N, P-CDs in the range of 5–30 µg mL−1, almost no bacterial colonies are found in agar plates after incubating with N, P-CDs at a concentration of 60 µg mL−1. This also confirms that growth inhibition of both bacterial strains was dose dependent and the effective antibacterial ability of N, P-CDs.

To gain more insights into the antibacterial effect of N, P-CDs, the bacterial morphology after treatments was observed by SEM. As shown in Fig. 5d, for the control groups, clear smooth surfaces and intact morphology could be observed in both typical rod-shaped E. coli and spherical S. aureus, respectively. Intriguingly, E. coli remained the bacterial membrane integrity after incubation with N, P-CDs, whereas the cell size was smaller than that of the control group, which is quite different from the most strategies reported previously [10,47,48]. This phenomenon excludes the possibility of physical damage on E. coli surfaces. We speculate that bacterial division and metabolism was suppressed due to the chemical effect of N, P-CDs. In contrast, the structural integrity of S. aureus was severely destroyed by N, P-CDs, the treated bacteria show obvious membrane damage and cytoplasm leakage, suggesting that N, P-CDs might cause a strong disruption on the integrity of S. aureus membranes based on various possible physical/chemical interactions. These results indicate further that N, P-CDs suppress bacterial proliferation of both E. coli (G-) and S. aureus (G+) through different mechanisms.

Nevertheless, limited by the resolution of SEM and the ultrasmall size of N, P-CDs, we cannot decipher the binding and adhesion between N, P-CDs and bacteria. To intuitively observe the binding process between N, P-CDs and bacteria, we also recorded the fluorescence images of bacteria incubated with N, P-CDs based on the intrinsic excellent multicolor labeling performance of N, P-CDs. As shown in Fig. 6a, CLSM images of N, P-CDs within the E. coli are clearly visualized in all three channels after incubation for 30 min, the intracellular FL signal remained stable and bright until 1 h later. This result revealed a rapid and efficient internalization of N, P-CDs into the E. coli. On the contrary, after incubating with N, P-CDs for 30 min, S. aureus cells display negligible FL signal under different excitation channels (Fig. 6b). Still, S. aureus cells all display noticeable FL signal after incubation for 1 h, suggesting a relatively slow internalization process of N, P-CDs into S. aureus cells, which agreed well with the bacterial growth curve results. This may be ascribed to the differences in membrane structures and/or composition diversity between G+ and G- bacteria [13]. Generally, S. aureus possesses thicker cell wall than E. coli, the thick peptidoglycan layer with negatively charged polymeric teichoic acids limits the binding efficiency between N, P-CDs and S. aureus [49]. To verify this speculation, we further recorded fluorescence imaging of B. cereus (G+) incubated with N, P-CDs. As expected, similar to S. aureus (G+), the B. cereus also show a time-dependent uptake process of N, P-CDs under the same condition (Fig. S8). Therefore, we can ignore the possibility of electrostatic interactions for surface adhesion between N, P-CDs and bacteria. Taken together, these results indicate that N, P-CDs could efficiently bind to bacteria as an effective antimicrobial, even if both the bacteria membrane and N, P-CDs are negatively charged.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Proposed inhibition mechanism of N, P-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: Besides the morphological investigation and fluorescence-staining analysis, the antibacterial mechanism of N, P-CDs against different bacterial strains is still unclear. To disclose the above phenomenon, we quantitatively determined the expression level of related genes by RT-qPCR assay to clarify underlying antibacterial mechanism. The RT-qPCR results were shown in Fig. 7.

Lipopolysaccharides (LPS) is the main component of the outer membrane of G- bacteria, playing a significant role in maintaining the permeability and stability of the outer membrane. The transferase WaaC and WaaF encoded by waaC and waaF genes catalyzes the synthesis of LPS, therefore, waaC and waaF genes play a fundamental regulatory role in the synthesis of bacterial cell membranes [50,51]. Here, the expression level of waaC and waaF had no significant difference compared to the control group (Fig. 7a), suggesting N, P-CDs do not affect the biosynthesis of LPS and cell membrane of E. coli. The filamentous temperature sensitive (fts) genes regulates the E. coli cell division process, where FtsA and FtsZ protein encoded by ftsA and ftsZ genes is essential for bacterial cell division [52]. Notably, the expression of ftsA and ftsZ genes was apparently down-regulated after treatment with N, P-CDs, indicating that N, P-CDs effectively inhibited the asexual binary fission proliferation of E. coli. RNA polymerase catalyzes the synthesis of RNA, and is essential for RNA transcription and genetic information transmission. Two related rpoA and rpoB genes encode the synthesis of a subtype of RNA polymerase in E. coli [53]. Similarly, the expression of rpoA and rpoB genes was down-regulated 8.93-fold and 4.27-fold, respectively. This indicates that N, P-CDs severely inhibit the synthesis of RNA polymerase, hinder the transmission of genetic information, resulting in the weakening of other functions in E. coli.

Energy metabolism is essential for bacterial life activities. Carbon storage regulator A (csrA) gene encodes the CsrA protein, which is a conditionally essential gene in E. coli and controls the entire metabolic network of E. coli [54]. Additionally, glucose is mainly absorbed into the cytoplasm of bacteria through the phosphotransferase system (PTS), further participating in and maintaining the physiological activities of the E. coli [55]. The ability of PTS pathway to transport glucose is partially regulated by ptsH and ptsI genes in E. coli [56]. We found that the expression of the csrA, ptsH, and ptsI genes was down-regulated 2.52-fold, 2.12-fold, and 2.43-fold, respectively (Fig. 7a). These results indicated the growth and energy metabolism of E. coli was significantly inhibited by N, P-CDs.

Inspired by the SEM characterization of S. aureus, we determined the expression of related genes to further explore antibacterial mechanism of N, P-CDs. S. aureus possesses an inherent ability to form complex multi-cellular structure (i.e. biofilm) on the surfaces, and the biofilm facilitates nutrient exchange and signal transmission within bacterial communities. The expression level of polysaccharide intercellular adhesin (PIA) is closely related to the ability of S. aureus biofilm formation, while the synthesis of PIA is regulated by the icaA gene. Therefore, the icaA is a key gene for biofilm formation of S. aureus [57]. In addition, accessory gene regulator (agr) of S. aureus also plays an important regulatory role in biofilm formation [58]. Meanwhile, the agr of S. aureus is closely related to the regulation of bacterial quorum sensing (QS), and agr operon mediates the expression of pathogenic factors in S. aureus invasive infections. For the QS agr system, agrA gene is in a critical position in the sequences of agr [58,59]. Excitingly, the expression of icaA and agrA was down-regulated 38.46-fold and 5.18-fold, respectively (Fig. 7b). This indicates that the N, P-CDs significantly inhibited the biofilm formation and signal transmission within S. aureus. Additionally, a classical crystal violet method was applied to quantify the biofilm biomass of two strains (Fig. 7c), the quantitative analysis was disclosed in Fig. 7d, N, P-CDs showed a weak ability to inhibit biofilm formation for E. coli, but the biofilm formation of S. aureus was significantly inhibited, which is in consistent with the aforementioned characterization results.

FtsA protein encoded by ftsA gene is an essential protein that maintain bacterial cell structure during bacterial division [60]. As shown in Fig. 7b, the expression of ftsA gene was down-regulated after incubation with N, P-CDs. Besides, two different DNA polymerase, PolA and DnaE, are indispensable for chromosomal DNA replication in S. aureus. Therefore, the polA and dnaE genes encoding PolA and DnaE protein are essential in the process of S. aureus DNA replication [61]. Notably, the expression of the polA and dnaE genes was down-regulated 5.05-fold and 16.05-fold, respectively. Therefore, these results verified that the DNA replication and cell division of S. aureus was severely hindered by the N, P-CDs. Moreover, As depicted in Fig. 7e, different from E. coli, leakage of intracellular DNA was observed in S. aureus, indicated that N, P-CDs destroyed the bacterial wall and membrane. To determine whether genomic DNA of E. coli and S. aureus were fragmented, we performed agarose gel electrophoresis (Fig. S9), the genomic DNA of E. coli and S. aureus maintained integrity after treatment with N, P-CDs, suggesting that N, P-CDs do not destroy bacterial DNA.

Taken together, we further confirmed that N, P-CDs against G- and G+ bacteria had different antibacterial mechanisms. As illustrated in Scheme 2, N, P-CDs mainly acted as inhibitor in E. coli (G-) to hinder binary fission proliferation and transcription of genetic information, interfere carbon metabolism and sugar conversion, thus inducing growth arrest and cell death. For S. aureus (G+), N, P-CDs mainly acted as QS inhibitor to disrupt QS system, inhibit the biofilm formation, DNA replication, cell division, and signal transmission, thus inducing bacteria lysis. These quantitative results were consistent with the SEM characterization results.

In summary, we prepared a new N, P-CDs using one-step microwave-assisted method with eco-friendly, low-cost mango peel as the precursor. The N, P-CDs exhibit tunable multicolor fluorescence emission in most of the visible region due to increased graphite content and the formation of more conjugated structures. Owing to the good in vitro biocompatibility and excellent stability, N, P-CDs can be applied as a practicable multicolor fluorescent label in bioimaging. We then found that the prepared N, P-CDs possess excellent antibacterial activity against E. coli and S. aureus, realized the transformation of waste into valuable multifunctional biomaterials. More importantly, by quantifying the expression level of related genes, we further confirmed that N, P-CDs against E. coli (G-) and S. aureus (G+) had different antibacterial mechanisms, which may hold great potential as antimicrobial agents for biomedical applications. Overall, the current work provides new insights into the interaction between negatively charged CDs and bacteria, as well the antibacterial mechanism of CDs at the molecular level.

Jianhao Hua: Conceptualization, Software, Formal analysis, Investigation, Data curation, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing, Visualization. Peng Hua: Validation, Resources, Supervision, Project administration. Kunhao Qin: Methodology, Software, Resources, Supervision, Project administration, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by grants from the Analysis and Testing Foundation of Kunming University of Science and Technology (No. 2017M20162118079), and the China Postdoctoral Science Foundation (No. 2018M643535).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.colsurfa.2023.132672.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:

.
